# Top 10 Legal Tech Training Programs 2024

###### 

Discover the top legal tech training programs of 2024 to enhance your skills in AI, innovation, and efficiency for a competitive edge in the legal field.

## Save 90% on your legal bills

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65f106bfe7e25afdb317678f/6642403c6f989dec37e5eb68_663c18d70eecf27455d25fb0-36dddae6d30512fbec47e20f7a5645c4.jpeg)

The legal field is rapidly evolving with technology playing a pivotal role. Legal professionals who understand legal technology are in high demand. This article guides you through the top legal tech training programs available in 2024 to help you stay ahead of the curve.

## Related video from YouTube

## Key Benefits of Legal Tech Training

## Top Programs at a Glance

| Program | Institution | Key Focus Areas |
| --- | --- | --- |
| LTC4 Core Competency Learning Plans and Certification | LTC4 | Legal technology workflows, industry certification |
| Generative AI for the Legal Profession | Berkeley Law Executive Education | Leveraging generative AI like [ChatGPT](https://openai.com/chatgpt) for legal tasks |
| Legal Innovation & Technology Certificate | Suffolk University | Legal tech tools, process optimization, project management |
| Professional Certificate Program in Legal Tech | MIT Professional Education | Legal marketing, client engagement, digital transformation |
| Law & Technology Certificate Program | Berkeley Law | Intellectual property, privacy, cybersecurity, emerging tech law |
| Legal Tech Skills Certificate | Maurice A. Deane School of Law | [Microsoft Office](https://www.microsoft.com/en-ng), [Adobe Acrobat](https://www.adobe.com/acrobat.html) for legal document workflows |
| Legal Technology & Innovation Certificate | LTI.Institute | Legal design, emerging tech, digital transformation strategies |
| CS50 for Lawyers | Harvard University | Computational thinking, programming, cybersecurity for lawyers |
| AI & Law | Lund University | AI's impact on law, legal responsibility for AI systems |
| Oxford Artificial Intelligence Programme | [University of Oxford](https://www.ox.ac.uk/) | AI technology, machine learning, ethical challenges |

## Networking and Collaboration Opportunities

By investing in these top legal tech training programs, legal professionals can equip themselves with the knowledge and skills necessary to thrive in the rapidly evolving legal landscape, while also gaining access to invaluable networking and collaboration opportunities.

## Evaluation Criteria

To identify the top legal tech training programs, we assessed several key factors to ensure a valuable learning experience:

### 1. Course Content Relevance

The curriculum should cover the most important legal technology topics, tools, and trends. Programs were evaluated on their ability to provide up-to-date, practical knowledge directly applicable to legal practice. Key areas considered include:

### 2. Hands-on Training

Theoretical knowledge is important, but practical experience is crucial for developing tech skills. Top programs offer opportunities to apply concepts through:

| Hands-on Learning | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Case Studies | Real-world scenarios to practice problem-solving |
| Simulations | Interactive exercises to mimic real-life situations |
| Capstone Projects | Culminating projects to showcase acquired skills |
| Software Access | Exposure to industry tools and software |

### 3. Instructor Expertise

Quality instruction from subject matter experts is essential. Programs were evaluated based on the credentials and experience of their faculty, including:

### 4. Industry Recognition

Reputable programs carry weight and credibility within the legal community. Factors considered include:

### 5. Networking Opportunities

In addition to skills development, legal tech training should facilitate professional connections. Top programs provide avenues for:

By evaluating programs across these criteria, legal professionals can identify training opportunities that deliver practical, career-enhancing legal tech education.

## Conclusion

The legal field is rapidly changing, and mastering legal technology is crucial for professionals to stay competitive and provide efficient services. The top legal tech training programs of 2024 offer a wide range of courses and certifications to equip participants with the necessary skills to navigate the ever-evolving technological landscape.

These programs cover various topics, from artificial intelligence and machine learning to legal analytics, project management, and blockchain technology. By combining theoretical knowledge with hands-on practical experience, participants gain a deep understanding of how to leverage legal tech tools and strategies to streamline processes, enhance decision-making, and deliver innovative solutions.

Continuous education and skill development are essential in the legal tech domain. As new technologies emerge and evolve, professionals must stay up-to-date with the latest advancements and best practices. These top training programs provide a solid foundation, enabling legal professionals to adapt and thrive in an increasingly tech-driven environment.

Moreover, these programs foster collaboration and networking opportunities, allowing participants to connect with industry experts, legal scholars, and fellow professionals. This cross-pollination of ideas and experiences enriches the learning experience and fosters a dynamic legal tech community.

In conclusion, the top legal tech training programs of 2024 offer a comprehensive and practical approach to mastering the intersection of law and technology. By investing in these programs, legal professionals can gain a competitive edge, enhance their skillsets, and position themselves as leaders in the rapidly evolving legal landscape.

### Key Benefits of Legal Tech Training

| Benefit | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Competitive Advantage** | Gain an edge over competitors by leveraging legal tech tools and strategies. |
| **Efficiency** | Streamline processes and enhance productivity through legal technology. |
| **Client Service** | Deliver faster, more responsive services to clients using tech-enabled solutions. |
| **Career Growth** | Develop future-proof skills and position yourself for career advancement. |
| **Innovation** | Stay ahead of the curve and drive innovation within your organization. |

### Networking and Collaboration Opportunities

1. **Connect with Industry Experts**

Engage with legal tech pioneers, thought leaders, and experienced professionals to gain valuable insights and mentorship.

2. **Build Professional Networks**

Expand your network by collaborating with peers from diverse backgrounds and jurisdictions, fostering potential partnerships and career opportunities.

3. **Join Dynamic Communities**

Become part of vibrant legal tech communities, facilitating knowledge sharing, idea exchange, and ongoing learning beyond the classroom.

By investing in these top legal tech training programs, legal professionals can equip themselves with the knowledge and skills necessary to thrive in the rapidly evolving legal landscape, while also gaining access to invaluable networking and collaboration opportunities.

--- SOURCE 2: Transform Your Law Firm: Master Legal Tech with Expert Training ---
URL: https://www.lexisnexis.com/en-us/products/interaction/blog/transform-your-law-firm-master-legal-tech-with-expert-training.page

SUMMARY:
Tailored legal software training for law firms ensures role-specific, hands-on learning, continuous updates, and fosters a culture of tech

FULL CONTENT:
# **success_training-blog-image**

![success_training-blog-image](/images/interaction/blog/6136885161-success_training-blog-image.jpg)

[by Drew Sammeth](#authors)  
September 17, 2024

In the legal industry, efficiency, accuracy, and compliance are non-negotiable. With the rise of legal tech, firms increasingly rely on sophisticated software to manage everything from case management automation to contact management and e-discovery. However, no matter how advanced the software, it’s only as good as the people using it… and that’s where training comes in.

## Tailored Training for Diverse Roles

Successful training recognizes that not everyone within a firm has the same needs. Lawyers, business development, legal assistants, and IT administrators will each use the software differently. Therefore, effective training programs should be tailored to distinct roles:

Generic training that doesn’t reflect the unique challenges of the legal industry will fall flat. Successful training is built around real-life scenarios that mirror the types of tasks users face. These tailored sessions make training relevant and immediately useful. This allows users to see how the software can directly improve their workflows while maximizing their use of the software.

## Interactive and Hands-on Learning

Legal professionals work in a high-stakes, fast-paced environment, where the consequences of mistakes can be severe. Training should go beyond passive learning, such as reading guides or watching videos. Hands-on learning, where users can engage directly with the software is crucial.

Interactive training modules, sandbox environments, and live demonstrations allow users to explore the software, make mistakes, and gain confidence in their abilities. By doing rather than just watching, users are more likely to retain information and feel comfortable with the technology.

## Continuous Learning and Support

Legal software evolves, and so do legal practices. To ensure long-term success, training should not be a one-time event. Continuous learning is key to keeping users up to date with the latest features and best practices. A successful training program offers:

## Metrics-Driven Evaluation

Successful training programs are not just about delivering, but also measuring effectiveness. A metrics-driven approach can help evaluate user engagement and knowledge retention. Post-training assessments, user feedback, and performance metrics can help track the program’s impact. Consider some of these common metrics:

These insights allow trainers to adjust future sessions, ensuring that users are not only learning but applying their skills effectively in their legal roles.

## Fostering a Culture of Technology Adoption

Lastly, successful training goes hand-in-hand with fostering a broader culture of technology adoption. This requires a holistic approach, where training is not just about how to use the software but why it’s beneficial. By making the benefits clear, training can help drive widespread adoption and ensure that users see the software as an essential tool, rather than a burdensome change. Trainers should highlight:

## Wrapping it All Up

Successful training is about much more than teaching users how to navigate the platform. It’s about providing role-specific, hands-on, and ongoing learning experiences that reflect real-life legal workflows. By measuring progress and fostering a culture of technology adoption, legal software companies can ensure their clients not only use the software but use it to its full potential—leading to better outcomes for legal professionals and their clients alike.

[CONTACT US](mailto:LNTraining@lexisnexis.com?subject=Training information request )

![Drew Sammeth](/images/interaction/authors/sammeth_drew_li_02.jpg)

Drew has been working in the legal marketing industry since 2007 and training legal marketing technologies for the last 8 years. Since joining LexisNexis InterAction in 2016, he has helped create customer-centric training content for industry leading software, that identifies customer needs and navigates them through a variety of learning options. He enjoys working with customers to ensure processes and strategies are efficiently trained and implemented, while advocating best practices and making InterAction a valuable firm resource.

--- SOURCE 3: The Power of Training: The Importance of Educating Lawyers on AI ... ---
URL: https://www.probonoinst.org/2024/08/28/the-power-of-training-the-importance-of-educating-lawyers-on-ai-tools-to-successfully-scale-pro-bono-efforts/

SUMMARY:
To successfully integrate AI tools into the work of law firms, firms will need to implement appropriate and ongoing training programs so attorneys can responsibly adapt to this new environment. For example, K&L Gates†, Orrick†, and Dechert\* each have instituted **AI programs for summer associates**, training them on the AI tools the firms use. * **Dechert**: Dechert runs **a one-on-one style training program** for summer associates with attorneys from the firm who regularly use AI tools in their work. * **Ongoing education:** As the nature of legal work continues to evolve alongside ever-changing technologies and generative AI capabilities, trainings must likewise respond with an emphasis on making lawyers’ use of these tools accessible and effective.

* [AI](https://www.probonoinst.org/category/ai/)

# The Power of Training: The Importance of Educating Lawyers on AI Tools to Successfully Scale Pro Bono Efforts

PBI Intern, Riley Kelly

**Background:** Advancements in artificial intelligence technology have the potential to revolutionize legal practice and, in turn, improve pro bono services. With legal service organizations being forced to **[turn away around 50 percent of potential clients](https://legaltalknetwork.com/podcasts/aba-journal-legal-rebels/2024/06/can-generative-ai-tools-make-it-easier-for-lawyers-to-offer-pro-bono-services/#:~:text=They%27re%20incorporating%20natural%20language,it%20might%20end%20up%20being)** who qualify for pro bono services due to a lack of capacity, pro bono services by lawyers and law firms are vital to narrowing the access to justice gap.

AI allows lawyers to significantly increase their efficiency when completing basic tasks such as reviewing documents and drafting letters, accomplishing **[in minutes what would take humans hours of time](https://legaltalknetwork.com/podcasts/aba-journal-legal-rebels/2024/06/can-generative-ai-tools-make-it-easier-for-lawyers-to-offer-pro-bono-services/#:~:text=They%27re%20incorporating%20natural%20language,it%20might%20end%20up%20being)**. By increasing productivity and efficiency so dramatically, the amount of time needed to assist clients on a per-matter basis may be significantly reduced.  By reducing time spent on basic tasks, lawyers can spend more time building human connections with their clients and devoting their efforts to tasks where their expertise is needed most, maximizing valuable pro bono resources. Law firms that leverage these emerging AI technologies will be able to offer **[lower cost, more efficient legal services with higher chances of favorable outcomes](https://www.brookings.edu/articles/how-ai-will-revolutionize-the-practice-of-law/)**. This is revolutionary in the context of pro bono, where significantly more clients in need can be reached.

**Challenges with Adoption in Legal Practice:** Generally, efforts to leverage emerging AI tools have faced some resistance in the legal field. One common concern is the **[risks involved](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4733061)** if these technologies are not used properly. There are ethical risks, such as data privacy and confidentiality concerns, as well as accuracy concerns caused by “hallucinations.” AI hallucinations are inaccurate or misleading results generated by the AI tool. They can be **[caused by several factors](https://mitsloanedtech.mit.edu/ai/basics/addressing-ai-hallucinations-and-bias/)**, including insufficient training data, false assumptions made by the tool, or biases in the data used to train it.

Also,  **[stark inequalities have been observed](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4733061)** in the adoption and use of AI tools. A recent study on AI tools indicated that men are more likely to be trusting and open to using these new tools than women. The study found that 47 percent of men reported using AI in their work, in comparison to only 17 percent of women. Women were similarly far less optimistic about the tools – **[50 percent of women reported that they were unsure of or were not planning to use AI in their work in the future, compared to just 28 percent of men](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4733061)**.

Many of the challenges to AI adoption, from distrust of the technology to accuracy concerns, stem from a lack of education around the technology. To utilize AI responsibly while maximizing its advantages, users must be trained and educated in it. **[For example](https://legaltalknetwork.com/podcasts/aba-journal-legal-rebels/2024/06/can-generative-ai-tools-make-it-easier-for-lawyers-to-offer-pro-bono-services/#:~:text=They're%20incorporating%20natural%20language,it%20might%20end%20up%20being.)**, users should understand the differences between consumer grade versus professional grade AI tools, should be trained to validate and verify AI generated answers, and should not view AI as a monolith. They must also be aware of current court rules and ethical obligations pertaining to the technology.

**Power of Training:** Having reached the point where AI proficiency is more widely recognized as a fundamental and valuable skill for legal professionals, dedicated training is necessary. **[Research](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4733061)** has shown that the way training is conducted and the way AI tools are introduced in legal settings matter.

Researchers at the **[University of California Berkeley Center for Law and Technology](https://www.law.berkeley.edu/research/bclt/)** and **[Miriam Kim](https://www.mto.com/lawyers/Miriam-Kim/)**, partner at Munger, Tolles & Olson\*, conducted a **[randomized controlled trial](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4733061)** to evaluate how the manner in which an AI tool is introduced impacts uptake rates and user experience. All participants in the study were provided 1-2 months of access to paid generative AI tools, but a randomly selected group of them also received “concierge support services” in the form of weekly emails with sample use cases, invitations to tailored trainings by representatives of the companies producing the tools, and weekly office hours.

While exposure to the tools **[seemed to benefit both groups](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4733061)**, with 90 percent of participants reporting increased productivity and 75 percent reporting that they intended to continue using generative AI tools, **[results](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4733061)** suggested that participants receiving the concierge services had significantly better outcomes in terms of productivity, satisfaction, quality of output, frequency of use, attitudes, and prospects for continued use than the control group. **[For example](https://www.law360.com/articles/1815450/study-sees-promise-for-gen-ai-tools-in-closing-justice-gap)**, 88 percent of the concierge support group reported a positive impact of the tools, compared to 62 percent of the standard access group.

The trial also showed promising results for overcoming gender disparities in adoption. While women were far less likely than men to use the tools before the trial, **[this distinction disappeared](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4733061)** by the end of the trial. All participants learned that they could **[manage risk concerns](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4733061)** by focusing the tools on lower-risk tasks such as document summarization, translation, confirmatory or preliminary research, preparing first drafts, brainstorming, conducting nonlegal writing and related tasks, and performing initial client intake functions. Participants reported about a **[50 percent productivity increase](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4733061)** on these lower-risk tasks. These results indicate the importance of the way AI tools are introduced to lawyers. Assistance and adequate training improve technology adoption because they help legal teams see AI as augmenting, rather than threatening, their work. This is critical given that using AI tools may become **[ethically required](https://www.2civility.org/the-ai-revolution-in-legal-and-the-billable-hour-is-the-end-near/)** in certain instances, as lawyers will not be justified in billing their clients for hours of work on a task that AI is capable of performing in mere minutes.

**AI Training for Law Firms:** **[Lawyers with AI skills](https://www.pwc.com/gx/en/issues/artificial-intelligence/job-barometer/report.pdf)** are in demand. Nearly **[73 percent of lawyers polled](https://images.go.wolterskluwerlr.com/Web/WoltersKluwerLRSUS/%7B0d5f497f-68b8-4d60-9dc4-6013e0528a9f%7D_FRL_LR_white_paper_FINAL_10-25-23_single.pdf)** reported that they plan to use generative AI in their work over the next year.  This means that lawyers will need to develop key skills such as choosing the right AI tool for a given task, engineering effective prompts, and evaluating the relevance, quality, and accuracy of generated responses. To successfully integrate AI tools into the work of law firms, firms will need to implement appropriate and ongoing training programs so attorneys can responsibly adapt to this new environment.

Existing examples of AI trainings for lawyers include workshops, seminars, and hands-on training on specific AI tools. These **[trainings should include](https://www.brookings.edu/articles/how-ai-will-revolutionize-the-practice-of-law/)** the basic mechanics of the tools, what services they should be used for, examples of prompts and questions to ask the tool, and how to address ethical risks involved. Several major law firms are ahead of the curve and are leading the way in implementing AI trainings. For example, K&L Gates†, Orrick†, and Dechert\* each have instituted **[AI programs for summer associates](https://news.bloomberglaw.com/business-and-practice/law-firms-start-training-summer-associates-on-using-generative-ai)**, training them on the AI tools the firms use. Summer associates learn how to use the research and chatbot tools used by the firm for tasks like legal writing, document creation and summarization, drafting, proofreading, and more. This development signals a commitment to new technology, paving the way for young lawyers to develop these critically important competencies.

* **[K&L Gates](https://www.klgates.com/)**: Brendan McDonnell**[, K&L Gates AI Solutions](https://www.klgates.com/artificial-intelligence#LangCode=en-US)** lead, highlights that it **[is important to teach people how developments in AI will change the way they work.](https://news.bloomberglaw.com/business-and-practice/law-firms-start-training-summer-associates-on-using-generative-ai)** Summer associates go through firm-led generative AI training, introducing them to **[CaseText’s Co-Counsel](https://casetext.com/?utm_source=google&utm_medium=paidsearch&utm_campaign=brand-cocounsel&utm_content=_&utm_term=casetext%20cocounsel&hsa_acc=1447382923&hsa_cam=19749619180&hsa_grp=146520618637&hsa_ad=649786144508&hsa_src=g&hsa_tgt=kwd-1969665058692&hsa_kw=casetext%20cocounsel&hsa_mt=p&hsa_net=adwords&hsa_ver=3&gad_source=1&gclid=CjwKCAjwtNi0BhA1EiwAWZaANI8zqjEcj0xww8CYZlfxiXbytUcdauQI9qb3Ld1w51KGhD-HK8vLIBoCcjkQAvD_BwE)**, a legal AI assistant used for case research and summarizing dispositions. Afterward, each associate is matched with a lawyer identified as an AI “power user” to learn to use the technology in their day-to-day work. This training is not limited to summer associates, K&L Gates is also partnering with the tech learning platform **[AltaClaro](https://www.altaclaro.com/)** to provide firmwide trainings on specific topics such as prompt engineering. **[AltaClaro has partnered with 15 Am Law 200 firms on generative AI trainings](https://news.bloomberglaw.com/business-and-practice/law-firms-start-training-summer-associates-on-using-generative-ai)**, showing the increasing prevalence of this type of education for lawyers.
* **[Orrick](https://www.orrick.com/)**: Orrick also utilizes AltaClaro training and **[incorporates it into summer associate training](https://news.bloomberglaw.com/business-and-practice/law-firms-start-training-summer-associates-on-using-generative-ai)**. Orrick summer associates receive prompt engineering and generative AI training, culminating in an **[“AI day”](https://www.dataplus.ai/posts/list-of-law-firms-using-ai)** full of related activities designed to educate the next generation of lawyers. Orrick intends to [roll out the training to additional Orrick attorneys in the future](https://www.law.com/legaltechnews/2023/08/09/orrick-trains-summer-associates-in-prompt-engineering-with-new-course-from-altaclaro/). As a firm, **[Orrick uses generative AI tools](https://news.bloomberglaw.com/business-and-practice/law-firms-start-training-summer-associates-on-using-generative-ai)** such as **[DraftWise](https://www.draftwise.com/)**, a drafting and negotiation assistant, **[Kira](https://kirasystems.com/)**, an AI contract analyzer, and **[Westlaw’s Precision](https://legal.thomsonreuters.com/en/products/westlaw-precision)**, an AI-assisted research tool. Orrick has also incentivized increasing AI familiarity across the firm by **[offering attorneys billable hours credit](https://www.dataplus.ai/posts/list-of-law-firms-using-ai)** for work on innovation projects.
* **[Dechert](https://www.dechert.com/)**: Dechert runs **[a one-on-one style training program](https://news.bloomberglaw.com/business-and-practice/law-firms-start-training-summer-associates-on-using-generative-ai)** for summer associates with attorneys from the firm who regularly use AI tools in their work. This allows the summer associates to develop a familiarity and competency with the tools while simultaneously learning how and when to use them for specific legal tasks. Dechert has also launched its own internal chatbot, **[DechertMind](https://www.law.com/legaltechnews/2024/03/05/inside-dechertmind-a-proprietary-suite-of-gen-ai-tools-for-law-firm-work/)**.

In addition to these individual examples, eleven global law firms have signed on to the **[Generative AI Training Consortium](https://www.law.com/americanlawyer/2023/10/04/norton-rose-hogan-lovells-9-other-global-law-firms-join-gen-ai-training-consortium/)**, the first of its kind, to build a series of interactive training programs in generative AI for lawyers and legal professionals. These firms receive early access to **[SkillBurst’s generative AI training modules](https://www.skillburst.com/genai)**, which aim to ensure that everyone, regardless of experience level, is able to understand and engage with these new tools, while understanding their risks and how to mitigate them. For example, SkillBurst developed a series called **[“Generative AI Fundamentals for Law Firms”](https://www.skillburst.com/genai)** aimed at addressing privacy and security concerns, implications of data bias, ethical considerations, and liability and accountability. **[Firms](https://www.lawnext.com/2023/10/major-law-firms-form-consortium-to-develop-ai-training-through-skillburst-interactive-subscription-access-available-to-other-firms.html)** that have signed on include Eversheds Sutherland†, Hogan Lovells\*, Norton Rose Fulbright\*, Taft†, Thompson Coburn†, and Womble Bond†. Non-member firms **[may also purchase access](https://www.lawnext.com/2023/10/major-law-firms-form-consortium-to-develop-ai-training-through-skillburst-interactive-subscription-access-available-to-other-firms.html)** to the programs.

**Takeaways for Successful AI Training by Law Firms:** For firms and lawyers beginning to explore the fast-developing landscape of AI tools and training modules, the following are some key takeaways for structuring a successful AI training program.

* **Make the training hands-on and human to human:** Learning from another person who can demonstrate the tool serves as proof that the technology is helpful to a person doing similar work, rather than just an abstract application.
* **Create a community for sharing AI practices:** By sharing advice on best practices and specific use cases, lawyers can learn from each other about ways generative AI can be applied to legal tasks to maximize productivity.
* **Demonstrate tools in context:** Trainings should translate to a lawyer’s work as directly as possible. Not only does this increase efficiency and enhance lawyers’ capability, but it also reinforces to young lawyers that these tools are “allowed” and encouraged by the firm for specific tasks.
* **Manageable time commitment:** Using bite-sized training modules that can be completed in short increments is an effective way to make trainings interesting and attractive to busy lawyers.
* **Ongoing education:** As the nature of legal work continues to evolve alongside ever-changing technologies and generative AI capabilities, trainings must likewise respond with an emphasis on making lawyers’ use of these tools accessible and effective.

As the legal community increasingly accepts the impact that generative AI is having on the practice of law, lawyers effectively adopting these technologies will reap the rewards of enhanced efficiency, accuracy, and productivity. But simply making generative AI tools available does not guarantee equitable or optimal outcomes. Comprehensive training and collaborative sharing of information are the keys to unlocking the full benefits of AI tools in the legal world. Law firms are leading the way by providing AI trainings to their lawyers.

**Incorporating Innovation into Law Firm Pro Bono Programs**

Law firms deploying AI trainings and tools to enhance their practice should consider the unique and impactful ways these tools can increase access to justice. Pro bono programs can greatly benefit from the efficiencies created by new technologies. As innovation programs develop at law firms, it is important to be intentional about ensuring that firms’ pro bono practices are brought into the fold. Getting lawyers thinking about the extraordinary promise of AI in our legal system early and often will foster the skills, creativity, and determination that is needed to help the next generation of lawyers continue to work toward bridging the justice gap.

† denotes a Law Firm Pro Bono Project® member

\* denotes a Law Firm Pro Bono Challenge® signatory

# Legal Training and Assessment: Programs and Resources to Supercharge Your Legal Tech Skills

Understanding technology is a valuable skill for lawyers. When lawyers struggle with technology, time and resources that could be better utilized are wasted.

Are you still spending too much time struggling with Word and not enough time creating great legal documents? It might be time to get some training!

Technology training will help you accomplish the tasks you have today, and it will also help you identify opportunities for improvement in the future. With ongoing training, you will start looking for solutions before you’re frustrated, overwhelmed, and out of time. This will lead to tailored solutions, better results, and a more rewarding experience.

## Training and Assessment Programs and Providers

**a. Custom Training and Consulting**

[Affinity Consulting Group](http://www.affinityconsulting.com/) – Practice optimization, tech selection, implementation, and training

[LawTech Partners](https://lawtechpartners.com/) – Tech selection, implementation, and training

[Savvy Training](https://www.savvytraining.com/) – Tech training and consulting

**b. Technology Skills Assessment and Training**

[Legal Technology Core Competencies Certification Coalition (LTC4)](http://www.ltc4.org/learning-plans/) – Assessment and training through coalition of legal tech providers

[TutorPro](https://tutorpro.com/) – Custom in-application assessment and training

**c. Develop Your Own Training Program with this Guidance**

[Designing Technology Training Programs that Work](https://legal.intelligentediting.com/blog/designing-technology-training-programs-that-work/)

[Structure and Style: Eight Tips for Better Training Material](https://legal.intelligentediting.com/blog/structure-and-style-eight-tips-for-better-training-material/)

## On-Demand Training Tutorials and CLEs for Using MS Word Effectively

**a. Using MS Word Effectively**

[How to Get the Most Out of Word](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sPCBmg5Ot8w) – Overview of MS Word tips, tricks, and customizations for legal practice

[Chicago Bar Association](http://lpmt.chicagobar.org/for-cba-members-only/how-to-video-library/) – On-demand tutorials on time-saving aspects of MS Word in the “How To Video Library”

[Wizardry in Microsoft Word](https://lextechreview.com/wizardry-in-microsoft-word-course/) – On-demand CLE by Chelsey Lambert

[TeachUComp, Inc.](https://www.teachucomp.com/courses/cle/learn-microsoft-word-for-lawyers-training-tutorial-course/) – On-demand CLE on MS Word for lawyers (and [Library](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCfSBAXs9vB_bOS3pqLD6BCQ))

**b. Ethics Related to Technology and Effective Use of MS Word**

[How to Comply with the Duty of Technology Competence](https://www.wordrake.com/tech-competence-webinar) – Recorded live for the Chicago Bar Association on May 5, 2020

[Learning the Tools of the Trade](https://www.wordrake.com/tools-of-the-trade?_ga=2.180461679.954257580.1611592639-403274583.1548878626) – Recorded live for LiteraTV on April 30, 2020

## Follow Workflow-Driven Legal Industry Guidance for Training

[The Effectiveness Project](https://www.wordrake.com/the-effectiveness-project) – a cross-functional, multi-national, industry wide collaboration with LTC4 intended to make the document drafting process knowable and repeatable and offer a variety of free and low-cost tools to improve the process

[Standards Advancement for the Legal Industry Alliance](http://www.sali.org/) – a collaboration trying to create a “common language” for describing different aspects of legal matters and develop interoperability standards for technology

It is never too late to learn something new! When lawyers become more proficient with technology and document creation, they save time and provide more value for their firm and clients. Improving your skills is a win for the whole team.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Customized training ensures relevance, addressing specific roles within a firm and utilizing real-life scenarios to enhance user engagement and immediate applicability.
Continual support is crucial as technology evolves; providing resources, updates, and troubleshooting helps users remain proficient and confident in utilizing legal tech tools.
Implementing feedback channels allows users to share experiences and suggestions, facilitating continuous improvement of training programs and ensuring they meet evolving needs.
Fostering a culture of technology adoption involves highlighting benefits, providing role-specific training, and demonstrating how legal tech enhances productivity and client service.
